coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence behaviour of supramolecular hybrids containing graphene quantum dots and pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanines and porphyrins
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Achadu, Ojodomo J.
Managa, Muthumuni
Nyokong, Tebello
coredata.dc:description: Novel pyrene-derivatized zinc and cobalt phthalocyanines (Pcs) and porphyrins (Ps) were immobilized on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form GQDs-Pcs and GQDs-Ps supramolecular hybrids via the π–π stacking interaction method. Spectroscopic evidence shows that the resultant hybrids were stable owing to the strong π–π stacking interaction between the GQDs and the respective Pcs and Ps, thus leading to the fabrication of interesting and functional supramolecular hybrids by taking advantage of the delocalized π electron systems of GQDs and the macrocycles. The conjugates showed increased fluorescence quantum yields for the Pcs/Ps, but decreased values for the GQDs in the conjugates. These novel hybrid materials could potentially be deployed for studies in both fundamental and applied perspectives due to the synergistic contributions resulting from the combination of their excellent electronic and optical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanines and porphyrins
Non-covalent interaction
Förster resonance energy transfer

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Nanocomposites of GQDs and ZnTPyPc (5) or CoTPyPc (6) and ZnTPyP (7) or CoTPyP (8)
Characterization of complexes 1–4 in the absence of GQDs
Characterization of nanocomposites of Pcs/Ps with GQDs (5–8)
Fluorescence behaviour of GQDs and their nanoconjugates
Optical and fluorescence properties of GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route of pyrene-derivatized Zn and Co phthalocyanines.
Metal insertion into metal-free 5,10,15,20-etra(1-pyrenyl)porphyrin (H2TPyP) to form CoTPyP (4).
Representation of the interaction between GQDs and the pyrene-derivatized Pcs and Ps.

FTIR spectra of (A) (a) ZnTAPc, (b) ZnTPyPc (1), (c) CoTPyPc (2), and (d) 1-pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde, and (B) (a) GQDs alone (b) GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5), (c) ZnTPyPc (1), (d) GQDs-CoTPyP (8) and (e) CoTPyP (4).
Ground-state absorption spectra for (A) ZnTAPc, ZnTPyPc (1), GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) and 1-pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde (B) CoTAPc, CoTPyPc (2), GQDs-CoTPyPc (6) and 1-pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde. Solvent: DMF.
UV–vis spectra of (A) metal free porphyrin (H2TPyP) and Co metallated porphyrin (CoTPyP, 4) and (B) (a) ZnTPyP (3) and GQDs-ZnTPyP (7) and (b) CoTPyP (4) and GQDs-CoTPyP (8). Solvent: DMF.
Raman spectra of GQDs, GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5), and GQDs-CoTPyP (6) showing the corresponding Raman shifts.
Transmission electron micrographs of GQDs alone (A), GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) (B), and GQDs-CoTPyP (8) (C) shown as representative of the others. Size distribution histogram of GQDs alone also shown.
Dynamic light scattering plots of GQDs alone, GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) and GQDs-CoTPyP (8) conjugates.
X-ray diffractograms of (a) GQDs alone, (b) ZnTPyPc (1), (c) GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5), (d) CoTPyP (4) and GQDs-CoTPyP (8).
Ground state absorption and emission spectra of GQDs at 340nm excitation wavelength. Solvent: DMF/PBS (3:1).

(A) Fluorescence lifetimes of GQDs alone and of the various GQDs conjugates with Pcs and Ps. (B) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs alone and upon coordination with the respective Pcs or Ps at 340nm excitation wavelength. Solvent: DMF/PBS (3:1).
Spectral overlaps between the emission spectrum of GQDs and the ground-state absorption spectrum of (A) ZnTPyP (3) and (B) ZnTPyPc (1).

Possible interaction pathways between GQDs and the Pcs or Ps leading to the quenching of the GQDs emission upon coordination. A=excitation, B=energy transfer, C=deactivation of exciton relaxation, D=possible stimulated emission.
Photophysical data obtained for the GQDs and pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanines or porphyrins nanoconjugates. Solvent: DMF.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in brackets are for Q-band of Pc/P alone.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence behaviour of supramolecular hybrids containing graphene quantum dots and pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanines and porphyrins

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rhodes University, Grahamstown 6140, South Africa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pyrene-derivatized zinc and cobalt phthalocyanines and porphyrins were immobilized on graphene quantum dots with increased fluorescence quantum yields for the Pcs/Pc, but decreased values for the GQDs in the conjugate.

Novel pyrene-derivatized zinc and cobalt phthalocyanines (Pcs) and porphyrins (Ps) were immobilized on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form GQDs-Pcs and GQDs-Ps supramolecular hybrids via the π–π stacking interaction method. Spectroscopic evidence shows that the resultant hybrids were stable owing to the strong π–π stacking interaction between the GQDs and the respective Pcs and Ps, thus leading to the fabrication of interesting and functional supramolecular hybrids by taking advantage of the delocalized π electron systems of GQDs and the macrocycles. The conjugates showed increased fluorescence quantum yields for the Pcs/Ps, but decreased values for the GQDs in the conjugates. These novel hybrid materials could potentially be deployed for studies in both fundamental and applied perspectives due to the synergistic contributions resulting from the combination of their excellent electronic and optical properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess excellent optical and electronic properties coupled with high photostability, aqueous solubility and bio-compatibility [1,2]. These properties have made GQDs attractive alternatives to semiconductor QDs for applications in fluorescence bio-imaging, sensing (biosensors and chemosensors), photocatalysis, drug delivery and as photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy (PDT) [3–6]. The presence of delocalized π electrons facilitates the interaction of GQDs with a wide array of aromatic molecules through non-covalent π–π stacking. The exploration of the potential of hybrid supramolecular assembles of GQDs and organic macrocycles such as phthalocyanines (Pcs) or porphyrins (Ps) is envisaged to considerably advance several areas of applications.

Phthalocyanines and porphyrins are structurally similar macrocyclic and aromatic compounds with unique electronic features such as high molar absorption coefficients, robust redox chemistry and ability to transfer and accept energy/electrons [7]. Over the years, Pcs and Ps have been deployed for various applications such as in organic photovoltaic devices, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, photodynamic therapy (PDT), and non-linear optical (NLO) materials [8]. The versatility of these compounds is rooted in their excellent optical and electronic properties which make them appealing tools for the construction of supramolecular ensembles and functional hybrid materials [9].

Nanocomposites of Pcs or Ps with carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), fullerenes (C60), graphene and graphene oxide have been widely explored in the literature with interesting applications in many areas [10–14]. However, not much is known about the characterization and photophysical behaviours of GQDs and Pcs or Ps nanocomposites. Recently, the fluorescence behaviour of GQDs in the presence of Pcs was reported [15]. Another study reported on the application of GQDs assembled with Ps as a probe for analyte sensing [16]. Fe (II) porphyrin loaded onto GQDs surface has been employed as a photocatalyst for the oxidation of alcohols [17]. GQDs and Pcs or Ps nanocomposites and their potentials are still relatively unexplored. Hence, it is highly imperative that more efforts be channelled in this direction.

In line with our interest and studies on nanocomposites of GQDs and Pcs or Ps and their potential applications, we characterized and explored the fluorescence behaviour of GQDs upon coordination to novel pyrene-derivatized Pcs/Ps (Zn and Co as central metals). Pyrene and its derivatives are known to have a strong affinity for carbon nanomaterials due to the presence of delocalized π electrons (sp2 carbon) in their structures [18,19]. Pyrene-derivatized Pcs have been reported in the literature with phenoxy or oxo bridges, hence disrupted π conjugation [20,21]. There is only one report on a mono-pyrene substituted ZnPc where the π conjugation with pyrene was not disrupted [22]. Tetra-substituted pyrene-derivatized Pcs where the conjugation is not disrupted are presented in this work for the first time. It is also the first time where Co is used as a central metal for a pyrene-substituted Pc. CoPc/P complexes are known for their excellent electrocatalytic behaviour [23]. CoPc derivatives have been employed as components for molecular materials [24]. The presence of Co as a central metal in Ps/Pcs will result in reduced fluorescence behaviour due to the paramagnetic behaviour of Co metal. However, we show in this work that CoP/CoPcs have Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) efficiency that is higher than those of the Zn derivatives. The synthesis of metal free tetrapyrene porphyrin (H2TPyP) ligand used in this work has been reported [25]. Zn, Cu, Ni, Pt and Au derivatives of tetrapyrene porphyrin (TPyP) have also been reported [25–27]. The Co derivative is reported in this work for the first time. Also, studies on GQDs interactions with Pcs or Ps bearing pyrene units are non-existent in the literature.

In this study, GQDs were non-covalently coordinated to pyrene-derivatized Pcs or Ps via π–π stacking interaction. The pyrene moieties on the Pcs and Ps could assist in the immobilization of the complexes onto the GQDs due to the delocalized π electron structure of pyrene. GQDs-Pcs/Ps nanocomposites could expand and improve on areas of applications in which other carbon-based nanomaterials and their nanocomposites have achieved immense success [9,28].

Cobalt (II) chloride, 1-pyrene- carboxyaldehyde, sodium acetate (CH3COONa), anhydrous potassium carbonate, anhydrous sodium sulphate, quinine sulphate, Zn tetraphenyl porphyrin (ZnTPP), Zn phthalocyanine (ZnPc), and dialysis membrane tubing (MWCO 1.5kDa) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Dimethyl formamide (DMF), dichloromethane (DCM), and deuterated DMF-d7 were obtained from Merck. Absolute ethanol was obtained from SAARCHEM. Column chromatography was performed using silica gel. All other reagents and solvents were obtained from commercial suppliers and used as received. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized following procedures reported elsewhere [15]. Zinc tetraamino phthalocyanine (ZnTAPc) and cobalt tetraamino phthalocyanine (CoTAPc) were synthesized according to procedures described in the literature [29]. 5,10,15,20-Tetra(1-pyrenyl)porphyrin (H2TPyP) and zinc 5,10,15,20-tetra(1-pyrenyl) porphyrin (ZnTPyP, 3) were synthesized as previously reported [25].

Excitation and emission spectra were recorded on a Varian Eclipse spectrofluorimeter. Ground state electronic absorption was performed on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer in the range of 300–800nm. Infra-red spectra were collected on a Bruker Alpha model FT-IR Spectrometer with platinum-ATR. 1H NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker AVANCE 600MHz NMR spectrometer in DMF-d7. Elemental analyses were done using a Vario-Elementar Microcube ELIII. Mass spectra data were collected on a Bruker Auto-FLEX III Smart-beam TOF/TOF mass spectrometer using dithranol as the matrix in the positive ion mode. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a time correlated single photon counting setup (TCSPC) (FluoTime 200, Picoquant GmbH) with a diode laser. Details have been previously described [15].

X-ray powder diffraction patterns were recorded on a Bruker D8 Discover equipped with a Lynx Eye detector, using Cu-Kα radiation (l=1.5405Å, nickel filter). Data were collected in the scan range of 2θ=10° to 60°, scanning at 1°min−1 with a filter time-constant of 2.5s per step and a slit width of 6.0mm. A Bruker Vertex 70-Ram II Raman spectrometer (equipped with a 1064nm Nd:YAG laser and liquid nitrogen cooled germanium detector) was used to collect Raman data as reported previously [15]. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were obtained using a Zeiss Libra 120 TEM operating at 80kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were done on a Malvern Zetasizer nanoseries, Nano-ZS90.

A mixture of zinc tetraamino phthalocyanine (ZnTAPc) (0.10g, 0.092mmol) and 1-pyrene-1-carboxaldehyde (0.094g, 0.41mmol) was refluxed in 5mL dry ethanol (EtOH) at 80–85°C under an inert atmosphere for 2days. The crude product was cooled to room temperature and washed, respectively in ethanol, methanol, acetone and n-hexane by centrifugation and was allowed to dry in air. The product was thereafter purified over a silica gel column using a DMF/EtOH mixture (2:1). Complex 1 yield: (40.5%). UV–vis (DMF): λmax/nm (logε): 724 (5.02), 648 (2.74), 396 (2.81), 360 (5.19). Anal. Calc. for C100H52N12Zn, C, 80.77; H, 3.52; N, 11.30. Found: C, 81.14; H, 3.97; N, 10.82. MALDI-TOF-MS (m/z): 1486amu; Found: 1490 [M−4H]+. FT-IR [ATR (vmax/cm−1)]: 3102 (CH aromatic), 1737–1635 (CN imine), 1455 (CC aromatic). 1H NMR (600MHz, DMF-d7): δ ppm: 11.10 (s, 4H, imine-H), 9.72 (d, J =9.3Hz, 4H, Py-H), 8.86 (d, J =7.9Hz, 4H, Pc-H), 8.72–8.67 (m, 8H, Pc-H), 8.56 (m, 9H, Py-H), 8.41 (dd, J =24.6, 17.0Hz, 6H, Py-H), 8.55–8.54(m, 8H, Py-H), 8.43–8.37(m, 9H, Py-H) (Scheme 1 ).
The synthesis and purification process of complex 2 was as outlined for complex 1, except that cobalt tetraamino phthalocyanine (CoTAPc) (0.05g, 0.98mmol) was employed (Scheme 1).

Complex 2 yield: (42%). UV–vis (DMF): λmax/nm (logε): 706 (4.25), 641 (2.47), 395 (3.47) 360 (5.18). Anal. Calc’d for C100H52N12Co, C, 81.13; H, 3.54; N, 11.35. Found: C, 81.90; H, 4.02; N, 11.05. MALDI-TOF-MS (m/z): 1482amu; Found: 1486 [M−4H]+. FT-IR [ATR (vmax/cm−1)]: 1728–1626 (CN imine), 1446(CC aromatic).

The synthesis of complex 4 (Scheme 2 ) was achieved by the metallation of H2TPyP as follows: In a 25mL reaction flask, the unmetallated porphyrin (H2TPyP) (0.047g, 0.042mmol) was dissolved in 15mL toluene followed by the addition of sodium acetate (0.31g, 3.78mmol), anhydrous K2CO3 (0.31g, 2.23mmol) and CoCl2 (0.19g, 1.07mmol). The reaction mixture was refluxed under argon for 24h, after which it was allowed to cool to room temperature, neutralized with acetic acid and washed thoroughly with water. The organic layer was separated and dried under anhydrous Na2SO4. The solvent was removed in vacuo and the crude product was purified over a silica gel column using DCM as the eluent.

Yield: (61%). UV/Vis (DMF) λmax nm (logε): 440 (4.27), 563 (3.86), Anal. Calc. for C84H44N4Co: C, 86.36; H, 3.80; N, 4.80, Found: C, 85.28; H, 3.39; N, 4.39. MALDI-TOF-MS (m/z): 1167amu, Found: 1165 [M+2H]+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocomposites of GQDs and ZnTPyPc (5) or CoTPyPc (6) and ZnTPyP (7) or CoTPyP (8)
ce:section.ce:para: The non-covalent coordination (π–π stacking) of GQDs to complexes 1, 2, 3 or 4 was achieved following published procedures [15] but with slight modification. Briefly: 2mg of GQDs was added to 0.5mg each of 1 (0.003mmol), 2 (0.003mmol), 3 (0.004mmol) or 4 (0.004mmol), in 5mL of dry DMF. The resulting mixture was ultrasonicated for 2h; followed by stirring in the dark for 48h. Ethanol was used to repeatedly wash the products so as to dispel the excess uncoordinated Pcs, Ps and GQDs by centrifuging, until the solution in the centrifuge tubes turned colourless. The Ps/Pcs and GQDs are soluble in ethanol while the conjugates are not. The solid products were dried under vacuum overnight at 60°C. Then, 10mL of dry DMF solvent was added to the resulting solid products followed by sonication for 10min. The homogeneous dispersions were stable for several days. The nanocomposites are represented as GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5), GQDs-CoTPyPc (6), GQDs-ZnTPyP (7) or GQDs-CoTPyP (8).

Fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF)) were determined by the comparative methods reported in literature [30], using the following standards: quinine sulphate in 0.1M H2SO4 (Φ F
=0.52) [31] for GQDs, ZnPc in DMSO (Φ F
=0.20) for Pcs [32] and ZnTPP in DMF (Φ F
=0.033 [33]). For excitation where GQDs absorb (340nm), fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) are represented as ΦF(GQDs), whereas for Pcs (630nm) or Ps (490nm) absorb, they are represented as ΦF(Pc/P) . GQDs fluorescence quantum yields upon coordination to form complexes 6–8 were determined using Eq. (1) [34]:
(1)

F ( G Q D s )

F ( G Q D s )

F

( G Q D s )

F ( G Q D s )
(the fluorescence quantum yield of the GQDs alone) was used as the standard, F (GQDs) is the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs alone and FConjugate
is the fluorescence intensities of the GQDs coordinated to the Pcs or Ps at the excitation of wavelength of 340nm.
PhotochemCAD software was used to compute the FRET parameters [35].

FRET efficiency (Eff) was determined experimentally from the fluorescence quantum yields of the donor in the absence (

where κ 2 is the dipole orientation factor (the value in liquid medium is given as 2/3), n is the solvent’s (DMF) refractive index,

where f QD is the normalized QDs emission intensity and ε Pc/P is the molar extinction coefficient (M−1 cm−1) of the Pcs or Ps and λ is the wavelength (nm) at the absorption maximum of the acceptor i.e. the Soret or Q bands.

Due to the paramagnetic nature of cobalt metal, the use of nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR) precludes structural characterization for both cobalt pyrene-derivatized Pcs and Ps. Thus, the structures of the compounds were confirmed by UV, FT-IR, MS spectroscopic techniques and elemental analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of complexes 1–4 in the absence of GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the synthetic route for the formation of ZnTPyPc (1) or CoTPyPc (2) following the Schiff base reactions between ZnTAPc or CoTAPc with 1-pyrene-1-carboxyaldehyde. The FTIR spectra of complexes 1 and 2 (Fig. 1 A(b and c)) exhibited the CN (imine) stretching vibrations (1635 to 1737cm−1 for complex 1 and 1626 to 1728cm−1 for complex 2) which are not observed in ZnTAPc (used as an example in Fig. 1A(a) or 1-pyrene-1-carboxyaldehyde alone) (Fig. 1A(d)). The FTIR spectra of the ZnTPyPc (1) and CoTPyPc (2) complexes (Fig. 1A(b and c)) showed complete disappearances of the NH2 (amino) stretching vibrations observed between 3316 and 3195cm−1 for ZnTAPc or CoTAPc (ZnTAPc used as an example in Fig. 1A(a)). This clearly points to the successful transformation of the four terminal amino groups of ZnTAPc or CoTAPc to imine functional groups in complexes 1 and 2, respectively.

The 1H NMR spectra of ZnTPyPc (1) and pyrene-1-carboxyaldehyde in DMF-d7 solvent are shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting information). The protons signal at 10.90ppm corresponding to the aldehyde proton of the pyrene moiety shifted to 11.10ppm in ZnTPyPc (1), Fig. S1A and as a singlet peak, which integrated to 4 protons corresponding to the imine protons of the four imine linkages of the Pc ring to the pyrene moieties. The protons corresponding to the pyrene unit (at δ=9.51 and 8.65–8.23ppm, Fig. S1B) were still observed for ZnTPyPc (at δ=9.72ppm and 8.66–8.37ppm, Fig. S1A). The proton signals of the Pc rings were observed at 8.86ppm as a doublet which on integrating resulted into 4 protons. Another group of protons signals for the Pc ring were observed between 8.67 and 8.72ppm as a multiplet which was integrated to give rise to 8 protons which is in agreement with a sum total of the 12 protons on the Pc ring. The pyrene protons integrated for a total of 36 as expected. The mass spectra of the complexes were in agreement with the expected structures as shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting information).

The absorption spectra of ZnTPyPc (1) and CoTPyPc (2) in DMF (Fig. 2 ) show the characteristic features of pyrene moiety with typical absorptions at 395 and 360nm. The Q band for complex 1 was observed at 723nm, Table 1 . The Q band for CoTPyPc (2) in DMF was observed at 716nm (Fig. 2B). The Q band for complex 1 is more red shifted than for complex 2, typical of ZnPc complexes compared to CoPc [38]. The B-bands of complexes 1 and 2 were more intense than the Q-bands because of the absorptions by the four pyrene substituents on the Pcs ring in this region (Fig. 2). Beer’s law was obeyed within the concentration range of 1×10−7 to 2.5×10−5 M (in DMF) for both complexes 1 and 2, further verifying the lack of aggregation by the respective synthesized complexes.

The synthesis of CoTPyP (4) was achieved by the metallation of the metal free 5,10,15,20-tetra(1-pyrenyl)porphyrin (H2TPyP), Scheme 2. Porphyrins (Ps) are characterized by a Soret or B-band usually observed at about 400nm and weaker Q-bands which are observed between 500 and 700nm. The four Q-bands of the metal free precursor collapsed into two Q-bands following metalation, confirming the successful insertion of Co as a central metal (Fig. 3 A). UV–vis spectroscopic analysis of CoTPyP (4) showed the characteristic Soret band at 443nm typical of Ps which is red shifted compared to that of the metal free porphyrin at 430nm (Fig. 3A) and that of ZnTPyP (Table 1). Absorptions occurring at 322 and 339nm are due to the pyrene substituents on the porphyrin ring. FT-IR spectrum of CoTPyP (4) displayed absorptions at 3306 and 2995cm−1 which are attributed to the aromatic CH stretching vibrations of the pyrene units and the porphyrin ring, respectively. The CC stretching vibration of the CoTPyP ring was observed at 1572cm−1 and CH bending vibrations observed at 1057–962cm−1, Fig. 1B(e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nanocomposites of Pcs/Ps with GQDs (5–8)
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 3 is a representation of the nanocomposites of GQDs and complexes 1-4 via π–π stacking.

Fig. 2 compares the UV–vis absorption spectra of ZnTPyPc (1) or CoTPyPc (2) with that of the conjugates with GQDs (GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) or GQDs-CoTPyPc (6), respectively). The Q-band for the GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) conjugate was observed at 711nm which is blue shifted by 12nm compared to ZnTPyPc (1) alone (723nm), Table 1. The Q-band for CoTPyPc (2) was observed at 716nm and blue-shifted to 706nm on conjugation to GQDs to form GQDs-CoTPyPc (6), Table 1, hence there is also a blue shift. The spectra of the conjugates at region below 600nm were greatly modified due to the presence of GQDs showing the incorporation of GQDs and the Pcs in supramolecular hybrids forms. Q-band absorptions of Pcs are sensitive to π–π interactions. It was shown previously that the strong π–π attraction leads to the formation of ground state complexes of Pcs with carbon nanomaterials, resulting in spectral shifts [39].
There were no significant shifts in the Soret bands for both GQDs-ZnTPyP (7) (Fig. 3B) or GQDs-CoTPyP (8) compared to ZnTPyP (3) or CoTPyP (4) (Fig. 3B, Table 1).
The retention of the pyrene substituents absorptions around 322 and 339nm further indicated the preservation of the Ps structural integrity due to the π–π stacking interaction.

GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) and GQDs-CoTPyPc (6) were further characterized using FTIR spectroscopy. The broad characteristic absorption band of the −OH groups (at 3378cm−1, Fig. 1B (a)) usually intercalated on the GQDs surface and dangling at the edges were retained in the conjugates (GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5), shown in Fig. 1B(b) as an example). Since the coordination between GQDs and ZnTPyPc (1) is via π–π stacking interaction, the integrity of the surface functional groups is expected to be preserved. Comparing the main characteristic vibrational bands of GQDs alone, Fig. 1B(a) and ZnTPyPc (1), Fig. 1B(c) with that of the conjugate, Fig. 1B(b), peaks corresponding to CO stretching vibrations ca 1640cm−1 (GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5)) which occurred in ZnTPyPc (1) at 1631cm−1, Fig. 1B (c), was retained and observed to be more intense for the conjugate. Peaks around the region of 1511 and 1540 are attributed to CC of the conjugates and ZnTPyPc (1), respectively.

Fig. 1(B)d shows the spectrum recorded for the GQDs-CoTPyP (8) (GQDs-ZnTPyP (7) shown in Fig. S3). As can be seen from the spectrum for GQDs-CoTPyP (8), there is a broad absorption band which corresponds to the presence of OH groups of the GQDs at 3336cm−1, (Fig. 1B(d)) this vibration occurs around 3378cm−1 for the GQDs alone, Fig. 1B(a).This vibration is observed at 3382cm−1 for GQDs-ZnTPyP (7), Fig. S3D(a). A Peak attributed to CO stretching vibration (ca 1632cm−1) of the GQDs also appeared in the conjugate at about 1611cm−1. This peak was observed at 1648cm−1 for GQDs-ZnTPyP (7), Fig. S3D(a). The CC and CC of the Ps were observed at 1572, 1057, 962cm−1 for complex 4 (CoTPyP) and 1506, 1032, 1084cm−1 for complex 8 (GQDs-CoTPyP), which could be clearly visualized from the both spectra, Fig. 1B(d and e). These bands were observed at 1512, 1108 and 958cm−1 for complex 3 (ZnTPyP) and at 1504, 1122, 986cm−1 for complex 7 (GQDs-ZnTPyP), which could be clearly visualized from the spectra ZnTPyP (3) and its conjugate as presented in Fig. S3D. These observations further demonstrated the formation of supramolecular hybrids of GQDs and the Ps.

The D and G bands of the conjugates of GQDs with CoTPyP (4) or ZnTPyP (3) shifted to higher wavenumbers by υ =10 and 14cm−1, 43 and 9cm−1, respectively (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3C). The shifts in the D and G bands of the GQDs upon coordination to the respective Ps as discussed above is an indication of new complexes formation as similarly discussed in the case of the Pc derivatives.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to determine the morphology and size distribution of the as-synthesized GQDs, Fig. 5 A. The GQDs were monodispersed with sizes in the range of 2–6nm (average size∼4.5nm) which is typical of GQDs [3,5,6]. The nanoconjugates of GQDs and the respective Pcs/Ps appeared to be aggregated, however, the GQDs nanoparticles could still be observed in the various TEM images (GQDs-ZnTPyP (7) shown in Fig. S3A). Pcs and Ps are known to show aggregation upon covalent and non-covalent interactions with QDs and nanoparticles, whereby the interaction between Pcs or Ps adsorbed or coordinated to adjacent QDs nanoparticles could lead to their aggregates formation. Aggregation by π–π stacking is common in phthalocyanines [44] and porphyrins. The visible particle-like dispersions of the GQDs in the nanoconjugates indicate the strong π–π interactions between the GQDs and Pcs or Ps, which we ascribe to be due to the excellent immobilizations of the Pcs and Ps onto the GQDs as a result of the high density of delocalized π electrons introduced by the pyrene moieties.

In order to ascertain the sizes of the nanoconjugates due to the immobilization of the Pcs or Ps onto the GQDs, dynamic light scattering measurements were carried out. The hydrodynamic sizes for the Pcs or Ps nanoconjugates were found to be higher than that recorded for the GQDs alone at an average size of 8nm, Table 1. The GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) and GQDs-CoTPyPc (6) nanoconjugates showed average sizes of 28nm and 30nm (Fig. 6 , conjugate 5 shown as example), respectively. Similarly, the GQDs-ZnTPyP (7) and GQDs-CoTPyP (8) displayed average sizes of 28 and 38nm, respectively (Fig. 6 and Fig.S3B). The average hydrodynamic size of about 8nm for GQDs alone is slightly larger than the size distribution acquired using TEM. Thus DLS results showed that there was increase in the sizes of the GQDs nanoconjugates which shows the formation of supramolecular assemblies of the GQDs and the Pcs or Ps.

Nanoconjugates of GQDs and the respective Pcs and Ps were characterized by XRD and the diffractometric patterns are shown in Fig. 7 . GQDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak at 2θ=20.1° which is ascribed to (002) Bragg’s reflection of the carbon in the graphene layers [45].

Diffraction pattern of GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) showed a broad ill-defined peak in the region where the peak for the Pc was observed, Fig. 7c. The displayed sharp peaks incorporated into the broad pattern of the GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5), Fig. 7(c), (some were present in the Pcs complexes alone, Fig. 7b), could point to the increase in size following coordination between GQDs and ZnTPyPc (1) or CoTPyPc (2) which is in agreement with results obtained from TEM and DLS measurements.

Non-covalent conjugates of GQDs-ZnTPyP (7) or GQDs-CoTPyP (8) showed similar broad diffraction peaks (Fig. 7(e), GQDs-CoTPyP (8) shown as example). The shift of the 002 peak from 20.1° for GQDs alone to 26° for GQDs-CoTPyP (8) and the sharp peaks embedded in the patterns (similar to that of the Pcs conjugates) is a clear indication of new complexes formation between GQDs and ZnTPyP (3) or CoTPyP (4). Similar observations have been reported for π–π interactions between GO or single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) with Pcs [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence behaviour of GQDs and their nanoconjugates
Optical and fluorescence properties of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ground state absorption spectrum of the synthesized GQDs (Fig. 8 ) exhibited maximum absorption at 334nm which has been associated with the π–π* transition of the sp2 carbon electrons of the graphene core of GQDs [46].

Fluorescence emission characteristics of the GQDs were excitation wavelength-independent with no shifts in the spectral width or emission maxima wavelength at different excitation wavelengths (figure not shown). Maximum emission intensity was observed at 445nm upon excitation at 340nm (Fig. 8). Photoluminescence (PL) properties of GQDs have been linked to the presence of the surface groups of hydroxyls and carboxyls localized within the planes and dangling at the edges of the graphene-like architecture of GQDs [46]. These groups and other intrinsic sites introduce surface defects or centres which facilitates the relaxation of excitons leading to radiative recombinations, hence fluorescence emission. However, specific evidences for the observed excellent PL properties of GQDs are still an on-going active research topic [1,47–49].

In this study, we investigated the fluorescence behaviour of GQDs upon supramolecular assembles formation with the respective Pcs and Ps complexes to probe the influence of the complexes on GQDs PL properties and subsequent elucidation of the interaction mechanism. The fluorescence emissions of GQDs alone and upon conjugation to Ps or Pcs are presented in Fig. 9 B. At similar excitation wavelength of maximum intensity recorded for the GQDs alone, the GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) or GQDs-CoTPyPc (6) conjugates displayed weak fluorescence emission (weaker in the Ps conjugates) (Fig. 9B). This observation is an indication of the deactivation of GQDs emission resulting from mechanistic pathways involving energy transfer from GQDs to the respective macrocyclic complexes. Similar observations have been previously reported in the literature where GQDs PL emission is attenuated upon coordination to porphyrins [16] and phthalocyanines [15], possibly due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET).

Fig. 9A shows the time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) traces of GQDs and selected conjugates, showing the shortening of GQDs lifetime following conjugation. The fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) and lifetime (τF) were measured to be 0.31 and 5.7 (ns), respectively for GQDs alone, Table 1. Generally, there was a reduction in the fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF(GQDs)) and lifetime (τ(GQDs)) of the GQDs (when exciting where GQDs absorb) upon coordination with the Pcs or Ps corresponding to the observed quenching of the GQDs fluorescence (Table 1). In particular, we observed some marked differences in results obtained for the GQDs-Ps conjugates when compared to the Pcs conjugates as will be further discussed below. The larger decrease in fluorescence quantum yields and lifetimes for Co derivatives (6 and 8) compared to their corresponding Zn derivatives could be related to the paramagnetic nature of Co, which encourages intersystem crossing to the triplet state, hence lowering fluorescence [50].

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) studies between the GQDs and complexes 1–4 were carried out to further gain some insights into the observed fluorescence quenching of the GQDs emission. FRET is a non-radiative energy transfer process which occurs between a photoexcited donor fluorophore upon absorption of higher energy photons and an acceptor molecule in close proximity to the donor fluorophore [36]. The degree of spectral overlap of the donor’s emission spectrum and the absorbance spectrum of the accepting species generally dictates the efficiency of the FRET process, as shown in Fig. 10 .

We observed suppressions of the fluorescence emission of the GQDs in their conjugates with Pc complexes which was similarly observed for the Ps complexes, Fig. 9B. However, the observed suppressions were markedly different for the GQDs-Ps and GQDs-Pcs conjugates (Fig. 9B). It has been reported in the literature that the presence of π delocalized electrons (in molecules constituting supramolecular ensembles with donor-acceptor relationships) is a driving force which dictates the extent of a FRET process between π conjugated molecules [43,51]. Complexes 1–4 possess a large number of extensive delocalized π electrons systems due to pyrene moieties which could further facilitate their immobilization onto the GQDs to form supramolecular assemblies via π–π stacking. Hence, energy transfer via FRET is a plausible mechanism occurring between the GQDs and Pcs or Ps complexes. There is a complete overlap between the GQDs emission spectrum and the Soret bands (absorption) of the Ps, Fig. 10B. Spectral overlap exist as well in the case of the GQDs and Pcs but minimal as compared to their Ps counterparts (Fig. 10B).

FRET parameters (including the fluorescence quenching factor, QF) were determined so as to evaluate the extent of the FRET processes (Table 1). The spectral overlap integrals (J) were evaluated using PhotochemCAD software [35]. J (cm6) was estimated to be 2.4×10−14 and 9.2×10−13 for GQDs-ZnTPyPc (5) or GQDs-CoTPyPc (6). For the GQDs-ZnTPyP (7) or GQDs-CoTPyP (8) conjugates, J (cm6) values were 5.2×10−7 and 1.0×10−7, respectively (Table 1). These calculated values further confirmed that the Ps complexes (3 or 4) have better overlap as can be seen in Fig. 10A.

R0(Å) was generally greater in GQDs-Ps conjugates compared to GQDs-Pc conjugates. From the calculated r (Å) values which are a measure of the center-to-center distance, the range 5.4–9.8nm was obtained in both cases of Pcs or Ps conjugates and falls within the effective energy transfer distance range of 1–10nm which has been described as a necessary condition for FRET to occur in donor-acceptor relationships [36]. FRET efficiencies were higher for GQDs-ZnTPyP (7) or GQDs-CoTPyP (8), which obviously resulted from the better spectra overlaps between GQDs and ZnTPyP (3) or CoTPyP (4) as compared to their Pcs counterparts. As a result of this, there is a more lowering of fluorescence quantum yields and lifetimes of GQDs in the presence of pyrene-derivatized porphyrin complexes as shown in Table 1. We also observed a higher FRET efficiency for the CoTPyP (8) conjugate with the GQDs as compared to the ZnTPyP (7). Higher FRET efficiency is associated with increased quenching of the GQDs emission as the quenching factors showed to be higher in the Ps conjugates with GQDs due to their very high FRET efficiency (Table 1). A possible interaction pathway between GQDs and the Pcs or Ps is further illustrated in Fig. 11 .

Supramolecular hybrids of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and pyrene-derivatized phthalocyanines or porphyrins were characterized and reported herein. Studies on the supramolecular assemblies of GQDs and phthalocyanines or porphyrins are still at their infancy. Therefore, a pathway to the formation of hybrids nanocomposites between GQDs and the macrocycles is presented by taking the advantage of the delocalized π electrons systems in both molecules. GQDs fluorescence emission was quenched as revealed by the spectrofluorimetric and time-resolved steady state measurements. Various spectroscopic techniques were employed to characterize the nanocomposites and the interaction mechanism leading to the quenching of the GQDs emission was elucidated via FRET studies. It follows that these novel hybrids could advance many areas of applications due to the excellent and optical properties of the GQDs (emerging carbon-based nanomaterials) and those of phthalocyanines or porphyrins which have been usefully harnessed.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology (DST) and National Research Foundation (NRF), South Africa through DST/NRF South African Research Chairs Initiative for Professor of Medicinal Chemistry and Nanotechnology (UID 62620) as well as Rhodes University/DST Centre for Nanotechnology Innovation, Rhodes University, South Africa.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2016.10.029.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
